meta.description: Comprehensive data model for 3D visualization of world religious and mythological traditions, their evolution, relationships, and influences.

dataStructure.traditions: Array of religious/mythological traditions with properties for visualization
dataStructure.connections: Array of directional relationships between traditions
dataStructure.syncreticNodes: Array of syncretic fusion points where traditions merged
dataStructure.scholarlyFrameworks: Reference frameworks for understanding religious evolution
dataStructure.timelineEras: Major historical periods for temporal positioning

traditions.name: Proto-Indo-European Religion

keyFigures.name: *Dyeus Ph2ter
keyFigures.cognates: Zeus, Jupiter, Dyaus Pita, Tyr/Tiwaz, Dievas

traditions.evidence: Reconstructed through comparative linguistics and mythology; archaeological evidence from Khvalynsk and Yamnaya cultures

traditions.name: Sumerian Religion

keyFigures.name: An
keyFigures.role: Sky god, father of the gods

keyFigures.name: Enlil
keyFigures.role: God of wind, storms, earth; king of the gods

keyFigures.name: Enki
keyFigures.role: God of water, knowledge, creation, magic

keyFigures.name: Inanna
keyFigures.role: Goddess of love, war, fertility, sex

keyFigures.name: Utu
keyFigures.role: Sun god, god of justice and truth

traditions.evidence: Cuneiform tablets, temple ruins at Eridu/Uruk/Nippur, cylinder seals

keyFigures.name: Ea
keyFigures.role: God of water and wisdom (from Sumerian Enki)

keyFigures.name: Ishtar
keyFigures.role: Goddess of love and war (from Sumerian Inanna)

keyFigures.name: Marduk
keyFigures.role: Supreme god, patron of Babylon, slayer of Tiamat

keyFigures.name: Tiamat
keyFigures.role: Primordial goddess of salt sea, chaos dragon

keyFigures.name: Nabu
keyFigures.role: God of writing and wisdom, son of Marduk

keyFigures.name: Ishtar
keyFigures.role: Goddess of love, war, and fertility

keyFigures.name: Ashur
keyFigures.role: Supreme national god, god of war

traditions.name: Canaanite / Ugaritic Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 3000 BCE (attested from c. 1400 BCE at Ugarit)

keyFigures.name: El
keyFigures.role: Father of the gods, creator deity, elderly wise ruler

keyFigures.name: Asherah/Athirat
keyFigures.role: Mother of the gods, consort of El, fertility goddess

keyFigures.name: Baal Hadad
keyFigures.role: Storm god, divine king, lord of rain, 'Rider of Clouds'

keyFigures.name: Astarte
keyFigures.role: Goddess of love and war (related to Ishtar)

keyFigures.name: Mot
keyFigures.role: God of death and the underworld

keyFigures.name: Yam
keyFigures.role: God of the sea and chaos

keyConcepts: El as supreme but passive god
traditions.evidence: Ugaritic texts (KTU), archaeological sites at Ugarit, Hebrew Bible references

keyFigures.name: Melqart
keyFigures.role: City god of Tyre, identified with Heracles

traditions.name: Ancient Egyptian Religion

keyFigures.name: Ra/Re
keyFigures.role: Sun god, supreme deity in Old Kingdom

keyFigures.name: Osiris
keyFigures.role: God of the dead, afterlife, resurrection, agriculture

keyFigures.name: Isis
keyFigures.role: Goddess of magic, motherhood, wisdom; wife of Osiris

keyFigures.name: Horus
keyFigures.role: Sky god, divine kingship, son of Osiris and Isis

keyFigures.name: Set/Seth
keyFigures.role: God of chaos, storms, desert, war

keyFigures.name: Amun
keyFigures.role: Hidden god, supreme state deity in New Kingdom (Amun-Ra)

keyFigures.name: Ptah
keyFigures.role: Creator god of Memphis, craftsman deity

keyFigures.name: Thoth
keyFigures.role: God of writing, wisdom, moon, magic

keyFigures.name: Hathor
keyFigures.role: Goddess of love, beauty, music, motherhood

keyFigures.name: Anubis
keyFigures.role: God of mummification and the dead

keyConcepts: Atenism (brief monotheism under Akhenaten c. 1353-1336 BCE)

keyFigures.name: Indra
keyFigures.role: King of gods, storm/warrior god (most mentioned in Rigveda)

keyFigures.name: Agni
keyFigures.role: Fire god, divine messenger, sacrificial fire

keyFigures.name: Varuna
keyFigures.role: God of cosmic order (rta), waters, oaths

keyFigures.name: Soma
keyFigures.role: God of the sacred drink, moon

traditions.name: Hinduism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE - 300 CE (formative period); roots to c. 1500 BCE

keyFigures.name: Vishnu
keyFigures.role: Preserver god (Trimurti); avatars include Rama, Krishna

keyFigures.name: Shiva
keyFigures.role: Destroyer/transformer god (Trimurti); evolved from Vedic Rudra

keyFigures.name: Devi/Shakti
keyFigures.role: Great Goddess; forms include Durga, Kali, Parvati, Lakshmi, Saraswati

keyFigures.name: Ganesha
keyFigures.role: Elephant-headed god of beginnings, remover of obstacles

keyFigures.name: Krishna
keyFigures.role: Avatar of Vishnu, supreme being in Vaishnavism

majorSects.name: Smartism
majorSects.focus: Advaita Vedanta, worship of five deities

traditions.name: Buddhism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 528 BCE (Buddha's enlightenment) or c. 5th century BCE
traditions.region: South Asia (spread to all of Asia)

keyFigures.name: Avalokiteshvara
keyFigures.role: Bodhisattva of compassion (Guanyin in China, Kannon in Japan)

keyFigures.name: Amitabha
keyFigures.role: Buddha of infinite light (Pure Land traditions)

keyFigures.name: Tara
keyFigures.role: Female bodhisattva, popular in Tibetan Buddhism

majorBranches.name: Theravada
majorBranches.description: School of the Elders; emphasis on Pali Canon, arhat ideal, monasticism

majorBranches.name: Mahayana
majorBranches.description: Great Vehicle; bodhisattva ideal, sunyata, new sutras

majorBranches.name: Vajrayana
majorBranches.description: Diamond Vehicle; tantric practices, deity yoga, mandalas, mantras

traditions.name: Jainism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 599 BCE (Mahavira's birth); tradition claims much older

traditions.name: Zoroastrianism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1500-1000 BCE (debated; some say c. 600 BCE)

keyFigures.name: Ahura Mazda
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator god, lord of wisdom

keyFigures.name: Mithra
keyFigures.role: God of covenants, light, justice (also in Vedic tradition)

traditions.name: Ancient Greek Religion

keyFigures.name: Zeus
keyFigures.role: King of gods, sky/thunder god (from PIE *Dyeus)

keyFigures.name: Apollo
keyFigures.role: God of sun, music, prophecy, healing

keyFigures.name: Aphrodite
keyFigures.role: Goddess of love and beauty (Near Eastern connections)

traditions.nearEasternInfluences: Aphrodite from Astarte/Ishtar, Dionysus has Near Eastern connections, Adonis from Canaanite Adon

keyFigures.name: Jupiter
keyFigures.role: King of gods, sky/thunder (from PIE *Dyeus via Greek Zeus)

keyFigures.name: Mars
keyFigures.role: God of war, agriculture (Greek Ares but more central)

keyFigures.name: Janus
keyFigures.role: Two-faced god of doorways, beginnings (uniquely Roman)

traditions.name: Norse / Germanic Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE (Proto-Germanic); peak literature c. 800-1100 CE

keyFigures.name: Odin/Wodan
keyFigures.role: Allfather, god of wisdom, war, death, magic, poetry, runes

keyFigures.name: Thor/Donar
keyFigures.role: Thunder god, protector of humanity (from PIE *Perkwunos)

keyFigures.name: Freyja
keyFigures.role: Goddess of love, fertility, war, magic (seidr)

keyFigures.name: Freyr
keyFigures.role: God of fertility, prosperity, sacral kingship

keyFigures.name: Frigg
keyFigures.role: Queen of Asgard, goddess of marriage, prophecy

traditions.name: Celtic Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 800 BCE (Hallstatt culture); literary sources c. 100 BCE-1200 CE
traditions.geographicOrigin: Central Europe, spread to Gaul, Britain, Ireland, Iberia

keyFigures.name: The Dagda
keyFigures.role: Father god, abundance, wisdom, life and death (Irish)

keyFigures.name: Brigid
keyFigures.role: Triple goddess of poetry, healing, smithing (Irish)

keyFigures.name: Cernunnos
keyFigures.role: Horned god of nature, animals, fertility (Gaulish)

keyFigures.name: Danu/Don
keyFigures.role: Mother goddess of the Tuatha De Danann (Irish/Welsh)

traditions.name: Slavic Religion

keyFigures.name: Veles
keyFigures.role: God of underworld, cattle, magic, commerce (cosmic opposite of Perun)

keyConcepts: Perun vs Veles cosmic battle (thunder god vs serpent)

traditions.name: Baltic Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1500 BCE (Proto-Baltic); last European region to Christianize (14th-15th c.)

keyFigures.name: Velnias/Velns
keyFigures.role: God of dead, underworld, cattle (cognate of Slavic Veles)

traditions.note: Baltic languages and mythology are among the most conservative of Indo-European daughter traditions, making them invaluable for PIE reconstruction

traditions.name: Early Israelite / Yahwistic Religion

keyFigures.name: Yahweh
keyFigures.role: National god, storm/warrior god, eventually sole deity

keyFigures.name: El
keyFigures.role: Canaanite supreme god, merged with Yahweh

keyFigures.name: Asherah
keyFigures.role: Goddess, possibly consort of Yahweh (eventually suppressed)

keyFigures.name: Elijah
keyFigures.role: Prophet who championed exclusive Yahweh worship (9th c. BCE)

keyConcepts: suppression of Baal and Asherah worship

keyFigures.name: Jesus Christ
keyFigures.role: Central figure, Son of God, Messiah

keyFigures.name: Paul the Apostle
keyFigures.role: Key evangelist, theologian of gentile mission

keyFigures.name: Mary
keyFigures.role: Mother of Jesus, venerated in Catholic/Orthodox traditions

majorBranches.name: Oriental Orthodoxy
majorBranches.description: Split at Council of Chalcedon (Coptic, Armenian, Ethiopian, Syriac)

majorBranches.name: Protestantism
majorBranches.description: Reformation; Lutheran, Calvinist, Anglican, Baptist, Methodist, Pentecostal, etc.

keyFigures.name: Allah (God)
keyFigures.role: Sole deity, same God as Judaism/Christianity

keyFigures.name: Muhammad
keyFigures.role: Final prophet, Seal of the Prophets

majorBranches.name: Sufism
majorBranches.description: Mystical/esoteric tradition within both Sunni and Shia

majorBranches.name: Ibadi Islam
majorBranches.description: Neither Sunni nor Shia; dominant in Oman

traditions.name: Chinese Folk Religion / Shenism
traditions.note: Chinese religion is highly syncretic; most practitioners blend folk, Taoist, Buddhist, and Confucian elements

keyFigures.name: Laozi/Lao Tzu
keyFigures.role: Traditional founder, author of Tao Te Ching

keyFigures.name: Zhang Daoling
keyFigures.role: Founder of Celestial Masters (religious Taoism, 142 CE)

keyFigures.name: Xunzi
keyFigures.role: Key philosopher, human nature needs cultivation

traditions.name: Shinto
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 300 BCE (Yayoi period roots); formalized c. 600 CE in response to Buddhism

keyFigures.name: Amaterasu
keyFigures.role: Sun goddess, chief deity, ancestor of imperial line

keyFigures.name: Izanagi and Izanami
keyFigures.role: Creator pair who made the Japanese islands

keyFigures.name: Inari
keyFigures.role: God/goddess of rice, foxes, fertility, industry

keyFigures.name: Hachiman
keyFigures.role: God of war, divine protector (syncretic with Buddhism)

traditions.note: Shinto-Buddhist syncretism (shinbutsu-shūgō) dominated Japanese religion from 8th-19th century until forcible separation (shinbutsu bunri) in 1868

traditions.name: Korean Shamanism (Muism/Musok)
traditions.originDateLabel: Prehistoric origins; attested from 12th century CE

keyFigures.name: Dangun
keyFigures.role: Mythical founder of Korea, grandson of Hwanin

traditions.name: Aztec Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: 1325 CE (founding of Tenochtitlan); inherits older Mesoamerican traditions

keyFigures.name: Huitzilopochtli
keyFigures.role: God of war, sun, patron of Tenochtitlan

keyFigures.name: Tezcatlipoca
keyFigures.role: God of night, sorcery, conflict, change

keyFigures.name: Mictlantecuhtli
keyFigures.role: God of death and the underworld

keyConcepts: human sacrifice to sustain the sun
traditions.note: Highly syncretic, absorbed deities and practices from conquered peoples

traditions.name: Maya Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 2000 BCE (Preclassic Maya); Classic period 250-900 CE
traditions.note: Maya religious practices survived colonialism in syncretic form blended with Catholicism

traditions.name: Inca Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1200 CE (Inca state emerges); roots in Tiwanaku traditions

keyFigures.name: Inti
keyFigures.role: Sun god, supreme deity, ancestor of Inca rulers

keyFigures.name: Viracocha
keyFigures.role: Creator god, Lord Instructor of the World

keyConcepts: three worlds (hanan pacha, kay pacha, uku pacha)
traditions.note: Absorbed local deities from conquered territories; syncretic survival with Catholicism in Andean folk religion

keyFigures.name: Orishas
keyFigures.role: Yoruba deities syncreted with Catholic saints

keyFigures.name: Shango/Santa Barbara
keyFigures.role: Thunder orisha syncreted with St. Barbara

keyFigures.name: Yemaya/Our Lady of Regla
keyFigures.role: Ocean mother syncreted with Virgin Mary

keyFigures.name: Ogun/St. Peter
keyFigures.role: Iron/war orisha syncreted with St. Peter

keyFigures.name: Sophia
keyFigures.role: Divine wisdom, whose fall caused material creation

keyFigures.name: Pleroma
keyFigures.role: Fullness of divine realm / the aeons

traditions.name: Manichaeism
keyConcepts: particles of light trapped in matter
claim to synthesize all prior religions
Mani as final prophet after Zoroaster, Buddha, and Jesus
traditions.note: At its peak, Manichaeism was one of the most widespread religions, stretching from Rome to China

keyFigures.name: Hermes Trismegistus
keyFigures.role: Legendary founder (fusion of Hermes and Thoth)

traditions.name: Greco-Buddhism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 4th century BCE to 5th century CE
keyConcepts: Greek artistic influence on Buddhist art
Milinda Panha (Greek king debates Buddhist monk)

traditions.name: Baha'i Faith
keyConcepts: progressive revelation (all religions from one God)

connections.evidence: Linguistic cognates (Dyaus=Dyeus), shared rituals, Rigveda hymns
Zeus from *Dyeus, cognate deities, shared mythological motifs
Jupiter from *Dyeus Pater, Italic branch of IE
Thor from *Perkwunos, Tyr from *Dyeus, runic traditions
Taranis cognate with thunder god, Lugus pan-Celtic
Perun from *Perkwunos, Veles from *Welnos
Dievas from *Dyeus, Perkunas from *Perkwunos, Saule from *Seh2ul; most conservative IE branch
Indo-Iranian branch; Ahura/Asura cognate, Mithra shared with Vedic Mitra
El-Yahweh merger, shared vocabulary, Ugaritic parallels in Hebrew Bible
Babylonian Exile catalyzed full monotheism
Angels/demons, eschatology, resurrection, Satan figure, heaven/hell dualism
Buddha rejected Vedic ritual and caste but shared cosmological concepts
Sufi mysticism influenced Guru Nanak's monotheistic vision
Interpretatio graeca/romana, wholesale adoption of Greek myths
Alexander's conquests, Greek art styles in Gandhara
Isis cult adopted by Greeks, Serapis creation, Herodotus correspondences
Hermes Trismegistus = Hermes + Thoth fusion
Silk Road transmission, Chan/Pure Land/Tiantai schools developed
Shinbutsu-shugo syncretism dominated Japanese religion for over 1000 years

connections.note: Also major Fon/Dahomey and Kongo influences

connections.evidence: Eschatology, angels/demons, resurrection, final judgment, Magi narrative
Shared Mesoamerican cosmology, Quetzalcoatl/Kukulkan, calendar systems
Neoplatonic concepts shaped Christian theology (Augustine, Pseudo-Dionysius)

syncreticNodes.name: Serapis Cult
syncreticNodes.description: Ptolemaic fusion of Osiris+Apis with Zeus+Hades+Asclepius+Dionysus+Helios to unite Greek rulers and Egyptian subjects

syncreticNodes.name: Gandhara Greco-Buddhist Art
syncreticNodes.description: First anthropomorphic representations of the Buddha using Greek artistic conventions; Herakles as Vajrapani

syncreticNodes.name: Interpretatio Romana/Graeca
syncreticNodes.description: Systematic identification of foreign gods with Roman/Greek equivalents across the empire

syncreticNodes.name: Roman Mithraism
syncreticNodes.description: Roman mystery cult drawing on Iranian Mithra but substantially transformed

syncreticNodes.name: Isis Mystery Cult
syncreticNodes.description: Egyptian Isis adopted throughout Greco-Roman world as universal mother goddess

syncreticNodes.name: Shinbutsu-shugo (Shinto-Buddhist Syncretism)
syncreticNodes.description: Kami identified as manifestations of Buddhist deities (honji suijaku theory)

syncreticNodes.name: Three Teachings Harmony (Sanjiao Heyi)
syncreticNodes.description: Chinese philosophical tradition viewing Confucianism, Taoism, and Buddhism as complementary

syncreticNodes.name: African Diaspora Syncretism
syncreticNodes.description: Forced syncretism of West African religions with Catholic saints during slavery: orishas mapped to saints

syncreticNodes.name: Andean Catholic Syncretism
syncreticNodes.description: Inca/Andean deities merged with Catholic saints; Pachamama with Virgin Mary, Inti with Christ

syncreticNodes.name: Maya-Catholic Syncretism
syncreticNodes.description: Maya religious practices survived colonialism blended with Catholic forms

scholarlyFrameworks.name: Axial Age (Achsenzeit)
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Period c. 800-200 BCE when parallel breakthroughs in philosophical and religious thought occurred independently in Greece, Israel, India, China, and Persia. Shift from localized concerns toward transcendence, critical self-awareness, universal morality.
scholarlyFrameworks.critiques: Debated: some scholars question the common denominator and the exclusion of other regions; others (Robert Bellah) have expanded the concept
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Temporal clustering node showing simultaneous independent religious innovations

scholarlyFrameworks.name: The Monomyth / Hero's Journey
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Universal pattern underlying hero myths across all cultures: departure, initiation, return. One underlying story structure connecting all world mythologies through a 'collective unconscious'.
scholarlyFrameworks.critiques: Criticized for oversimplification, Western bias, ignoring cultural specificity; largely rejected in contemporary academic religious studies but enormously influential in popular culture
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Cross-cultural thematic connection layer showing shared mythological patterns

scholarlyFrameworks.name: The Sacred and the Profane
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Religion emerges from experiences of hierophany (manifestation of the Sacred). Sacred space and time give structure and orientation to the world. Myths provide models for cosmic order. Builds on Rudolf Otto's concept of the numinous.
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Conceptual framework for understanding what all these traditions share: responses to the sacred

scholarlyFrameworks.name: Tripartite Ideology
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Indo-European societies organized around three functions: sovereignty (priests/kings), military force (warriors), and fertility/production (farmers/herders). These three functions are reflected in the pantheon structure.
scholarlyFrameworks.examples: Vedic: Mitra-Varuna / Indra / Ashvins. Norse: Odin-Tyr / Thor / Freyr-Freyja. Roman: Jupiter / Mars / Quirinus.
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Structural analysis layer for Indo-European traditions showing parallel pantheon organization

scholarlyFrameworks.name: Historical-Linguistic Approach to Mythology
scholarlyFrameworks.scholars: Max Muller (19th c.), Calvert Watkins, J.P. Mallory, Michael Witzel
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Uses linguistic cognates to reconstruct proto-mythological concepts. Cognate deity names (Zeus/Jupiter/Dyaus/Tyr/Dievas) prove common origin. Literary formulas and mythological motifs can be traced through comparative philology of Indo-European daughter languages.
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Edge annotations showing linguistic evidence for connections between traditions

scholarlyFrameworks.name: Archaeological Evidence for Religious Evolution
scholarlyFrameworks.description: Material culture (temples, ritual objects, burial practices, iconography) provides evidence for religious practices independent of texts. Key sites: Gobekli Tepe (c. 9500 BCE, earliest known monumental religious site), Catalhoyuk, Ugarit, Khvalynsk (proto-IE sacrificial evidence), Indus Valley seal iconography.
scholarlyFrameworks.useForVisualization: Geographic anchor points and temporal markers grounded in physical evidence

theologicalTypeDefinitions.henotheism: Worship of one god without denying existence of others
theologicalTypeDefinitions.monolatry: Exclusive worship of one god while acknowledging others exist
theologicalTypeDefinitions.pantheism: God is identical with the universe/nature
theologicalTypeDefinitions.panentheism: God encompasses and transcends the universe
theologicalTypeDefinitions.monism: Ultimate reality is one unified substance
theologicalTypeDefinitions.nontheism: Neither affirms nor denies deities; focus on practice/philosophy
theologicalTypeDefinitions.animism: Belief that all things (including nature) possess spirits
theologicalTypeDefinitions.shamanism: Spiritual practice involving altered states to communicate with spirit world
theologicalTypeDefinitions.ancestor_worship: Veneration of deceased family members as spiritual entities

visualizationSuggestions.temporalAxis: Use Y-axis or spiral for time (-5000 BCE to present)
visualizationSuggestions.geographicClustering: Group nodes by geographic region in 3D space

connectionTypes.influence: Dashed lines with arrows showing direction
connectionTypes.cognate: Thin dotted lines for linguistic/mythological cognates

visualizationSuggestions.nodeSize: Scale by number of current adherents or historical peak influence
visualizationSuggestions.nodeColor: Color by theologicalType family (polytheistic=warm, monotheistic=cool, nontheistic=neutral)
visualizationSuggestions.axialAgeHighlight: Visual band or glow at 800-200 BCE showing simultaneous developments
visualizationSuggestions.syncreticNodesGlow: Special visual treatment for fusion/syncretism points
